title: Beyond Text — Multimodal AI and Vision
subtitle: How the Transformer learned to see, read, and talk in one mind
theme: One architecture, many senses: the unification of perception and language
learningObjectives: Explain how the Vision Transformer (ViT) turns an image into a sequence of tokens, just like words
Describe contrastive learning and how CLIP aligns images and text in a shared embedding space
Understand how modern multimodal LLMs (GPT-4V / Gemini / Claude style) fuse vision and language into one model
Trace the pipeline of how images, audio, and other signals become tokens a Transformer can reason over
Reason about vision-language tasks: captioning, VQA, document understanding, and grounded reasoning
Connect multimodality to the June 2026 market where 'one model that sees and talks' is the default
storyHook: For sixty years, computer vision and natural language were two different kingdoms, with different armies, different maps, and a wall between them. Vision people stacked convolutions; language people stacked recurrences. Then in 2020, a quiet paper asked a heretical question: what if an image is just a sentence made of patches? The wall didn't crack — it dissolved. Today the same architecture that finished your email can describe a chest X-ray, read a handwritten receipt, and watch a video. This is the day the machine grew eyes.

slides.title: The Two Kingdoms, and the Wall Between Them
slides.body:
## Where we have been

For twelve days we have lived almost entirely in the world of **text**. We watched attention dethrone recurrence (Day 1), watched GPT scale into a generalist, watched models learn to follow instructions and hold conversations. Everything was tokens of language going in, tokens of language coming out.

But the world is not made of text. The world is made of **light, sound, motion, and structure**. A doctor reads a scan. A driver watches the road. A child learns the word 'dog' by pointing at a dog. Intelligence that cannot perceive is intelligence locked in a library with no windows.

## Two separate sciences

Until very recently, AI reflected this divide with two almost entirely separate research traditions:

- **Computer Vision** — built on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Think AlexNet (2012), ResNet (2015). These networks slide little filters across an image, detecting edges, then textures, then shapes, then objects. Brilliant at pixels, blind to language.
- **Natural Language Processing** — built first on RNNs/LSTMs, then on Transformers. Brilliant at words, blind to pixels.

The two fields had different conferences, different jargon, different heroes. A vision researcher and an NLP researcher could sit at the same lunch table and barely share a vocabulary.

## Today's promise

Today we tell the story of how that wall came down — how the **Transformer**, the engine built for language, turned out to be a *universal* engine for perception. By the end you will understand why, in 2026, a single model can look at a photo of your fridge and write you a recipe.
slides.speakerNotes: Open by physically gesturing to a 'wall' in the air between two imagined groups. Ask the class: 'How many of you have used an AI that can SEE — upload a photo and ask about it?' Almost every hand will go up. Then say: 'Five years ago, that was science fiction. Today let's understand exactly how it works.' Emphasize the emotional point: perception is what turns a chatbot into something that shares our world.
slides.marketConnection: As of June 2026, multimodal input is no longer a premium add-on — every flagship model (Claude, GPT, Gemini) accepts images by default, and 'text-only' models are increasingly seen as legacy.

slides.title: The Heresy: An Image Is Just a Sentence of Patches
slides.body:
## The question that broke the wall

In 2020, a Google team published *"An Image Is Worth 16x16 Words"* — introducing the **Vision Transformer (ViT)**. The title is the whole idea: treat an image like text.

For decades, the assumption was that images *need* convolutions because images have a special spatial structure — neighboring pixels matter, and a cat is a cat whether it's in the corner or the center. CNNs bake those assumptions in. ViT threw them out and asked: what if we just chop the image into patches and feed them to a plain Transformer, like words in a sentence?

## How ViT works, step by step

```
  Original image (224 x 224 pixels)
           |
   chop into a grid of patches (e.g. 16x16 px each)
           |
   = 196 patches  (14 x 14 grid)
           |
   flatten each patch -> a vector of numbers
           |
   linear projection -> a 'patch embedding' (like a word embedding!)
           |
   + positional embedding (so the model knows WHERE each patch was)
           |
   [CLS] token  patch1  patch2  patch3 ... patch196
           |
   ====>  standard Transformer encoder  <====
           |
   self-attention lets every patch 'look at' every other patch
           |
   the [CLS] token's output -> classification / features
```

That's it. **Patches become tokens.** Position embeddings tell the model the spatial layout. Self-attention lets the model relate a patch in the top-left to a patch in the bottom-right *in a single step* — something a CNN can only do after many layers.

## Why this was shocking

With enough data, ViT **matched or beat** the best CNNs. The 'special structure' of images that everyone thought was essential turned out to be learnable. The Transformer didn't need hand-designed vision priors; given enough examples, it discovered the right way to see.

The lesson echoes Day 1: **general architecture + scale + data beats clever hand-crafted structure.** The bitter lesson strikes again.
slides.speakerNotes: Draw the patch grid on the board live — a square cut into 9 or 16 smaller squares. Number them. Say 'these are now your words.' Ask the class: 'If I shuffle the patches randomly, can the model still tell what it is?' (Answer: much worse — that's why positional embeddings matter, exactly like word order in a sentence.) Stress the philosophical punchline: we kept thinking vision was special; it wasn't.
slides.marketConnection: ViT-style backbones now power most production vision systems in 2026, from medical imaging startups to the vision encoders inside every major multimodal LLM.

slides.title: CLIP — Teaching Pictures and Words to Speak the Same Language
slides.body:
## The matchmaking problem

ViT taught a Transformer to see. But seeing isn't understanding. To say *'this is a photo of a dog wearing sunglasses,'* the model must connect the **pixels** to the **words**. How do you teach a machine that an image and a caption mean the same thing?

In 2021, OpenAI's **CLIP** (Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training) gave a beautiful answer, and it changed everything.

## The idea: a shared meaning-space

Imagine a giant map of meaning. On this map, the *photo* of a beach and the *phrase* 'a sunny beach' should land in the same neighborhood — even though one is pixels and the other is letters. CLIP builds exactly such a map.

It has two encoders:

- An **image encoder** (a ViT or CNN) that turns any image into a vector.
- A **text encoder** (a Transformer) that turns any caption into a vector.

Both produce vectors in the **same embedding space**. Training tries to make matching image-text pairs point the same direction, and mismatched pairs point apart.

## Contrastive learning, intuitively

CLIP was trained on ~400 million (image, caption) pairs scraped from the internet. For each batch:

```
         caption_1  caption_2  caption_3 ... caption_N
image_1  [  HIGH  ]   low        low          low
image_2    low      [ HIGH ]     low          low
image_3    low        low      [ HIGH ]       low
  ...
image_N    low        low        low       [ HIGH ]
```

The model is shown N images and N captions and asked: **match them up.** Pull each image toward its true caption (the diagonal) and push it away from all the wrong captions. Do this 400 million times and the two encoders learn a shared language of meaning.

## The superpower: zero-shot recognition

Here's the magic. After training, you can classify an image into categories it was **never explicitly trained on**. Want to detect 'a corgi'? Just encode the text 'a photo of a corgi' and see if the image vector points the same way. No retraining, no labeled corgi dataset. You describe the category *in words* and CLIP finds it.

This 'open-vocabulary' ability is why CLIP became the connective tissue of the multimodal era — it shows up inside image generators (Day 14!), search engines, content moderation, and the vision encoders of multimodal LLMs.
slides.speakerNotes: Use the dating-app analogy: CLIP is a matchmaker that learns to pair photos with their right description by seeing millions of right and wrong matches. Ask the class: 'How would you classify an animal you've never labeled before, just by describing it?' Let them sit in the surprise that you can classify by *writing a sentence*. This is the conceptual bridge to everything multimodal.
slides.marketConnection: CLIP-style embeddings power semantic image search, visual recommendation, and safety filters across 2026 products; 'embed everything into one space' is now standard infrastructure thinking.

slides.title: From Two Encoders to One Mind — Multimodal LLMs
slides.body:
## The next leap

CLIP could *match* images and text, but it couldn't *talk*. It could tell you 'this image is close to the phrase corgi,' but it couldn't write a paragraph about the corgi, answer a follow-up question, or reason step-by-step about what's in the picture.

The breakthrough of 2023–2024 was fusing vision directly into a large language model — producing **multimodal LLMs** like GPT-4V (Vision), Gemini, and Claude with vision. Now you can paste an image into a chat and have a real conversation about it.

## The recipe: bolt eyes onto a brain

The dominant pattern is surprisingly elegant:

```
  Image
    |
  Vision Encoder (a ViT, often CLIP-trained)
    |
  produces image 'tokens' (vectors)
    |
  Projector / adapter  (a small bridge network)
    |
  image tokens reshaped to LOOK like word tokens
    |
  +-----------------------------------------------+
  |  Large Language Model (the Transformer brain) |
  |  sees: [image tokens] + [your text tokens]    |
  +-----------------------------------------------+
    |
  text answer streams out
```

The key insight: once the vision encoder turns an image into a sequence of vectors, you can **shove those vectors into the LLM's input alongside the text tokens.** To the language model, the image is just more tokens to attend to. The same self-attention that relates word-to-word now relates word-to-pixel-region.

## Why this is so powerful

The LLM already knows how to reason, explain, follow instructions, and write. By teaching it to *also* read these image tokens, all of that intelligence transfers to vision instantly. The model can:

- **Caption**: describe what it sees.
- **Answer questions** (VQA): 'How many people are in this photo?'
- **Read**: extract text from a screenshot or scanned document (OCR-by-reasoning).
- **Reason visually**: 'Why is this meme funny?' / 'What's wrong with this circuit diagram?'
- **Ground language**: point to *where* in the image the answer is.

One model. One conversation. Eyes and language unified.
slides.speakerNotes: The core metaphor: we already built a brilliant brain (the LLM). Now we bolt on an eye (the vision encoder) and a nerve (the projector) that translates what the eye sees into the language the brain already understands. Ask the class: 'Why is it smart to reuse the LLM instead of training a vision-reasoner from scratch?' (Answer: all the reasoning, world knowledge, and language ability comes for free.) This is transfer learning at the grandest scale.
slides.marketConnection: By June 2026, 'chat with an image/PDF/screenshot' is a baseline expectation; enterprise document-understanding and visual agents are built directly on these fused multimodal LLMs.

slides.title: Everything Is Tokens — Audio, Video, and Beyond
slides.body:
## The unifying trick

Once you accept that an image can become tokens, a thrilling generalization follows: **almost any signal can become tokens.** And anything that becomes tokens can ride the Transformer.

This is arguably the deepest idea of the multimodal era: **tokenization is the universal adapter.** Different senses, one socket.

```
  TEXT   ->  subword tokens         (the original)
  IMAGE  ->  patch tokens           (ViT)
  AUDIO  ->  spectrogram patches /  (Whisper-style)
             discrete audio codes
  VIDEO  ->  spatio-temporal patches (space + time cubes)
  3D / point clouds, sensor data, even protein sequences -> tokens
             |
             v
        +----------------------+
        |   TRANSFORMER        |
        |  (attention over     |
        |   any token stream)  |
        +----------------------+
```

## Audio becomes tokens

Sound is a wave. Turn it into a **spectrogram** — a picture of which frequencies are loud over time — and suddenly audio looks like an image you can patch-and-tokenize. This is roughly how OpenAI's **Whisper** transcribes speech, and how audio gets fed into multimodal models for voice conversations. Alternatively, audio can be compressed into **discrete codes** (like a vocabulary of sound chunks) that the model predicts just like words.

## Video becomes tokens

Video is images through time. Chop it into **3D patches** — little cubes spanning a small region of the frame across a few frames — and you get spatio-temporal tokens. The Transformer attends across both *space* (what's in the frame) and *time* (how it changes). This is the foundation for video understanding and, as we'll see tomorrow, video generation.

## Why this matters

The profound consequence: we no longer need a fundamentally different architecture per sense. **One blueprint — attention over tokens — absorbs the whole sensory world.** That is why progress in one modality now spills into all the others, and why a single company can ship a model that sees, hears, reads, and speaks.
slides.speakerNotes: Hammer the central thesis: 'tokens are the universal language.' Whatever you can chop into a sequence of vectors, the Transformer can learn. Ask the class to brainstorm: 'What ELSE could you tokenize?' (DNA, stock ticks, robot sensor streams, music scores, code...). Mention that DeepMind's Gato and similar 'generalist agents' literally tokenize game controls and robot actions too. The point lands emotionally: this is a general theory of perception.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, real-time voice assistants and video-understanding agents are mainstream precisely because audio and video reduced to the same token-and-attention recipe as text.

slides.title: A Day in the Life of an Image (End-to-End Walkthrough)
slides.body:
## Follow one photo through the machine

Let's make it concrete. You snap a photo of a whiteboard covered in messy handwriting and a hand-drawn flowchart, and you ask: *'Turn this whiteboard into clean meeting notes and tell me which action item has no owner.'* Watch what happens inside.

**1. The image arrives.** Your 4000x3000 phone photo is resized to the model's expected resolution (say 1024x1024 or tiled into several crops for detail).

**2. Patchification.** The image is sliced into hundreds of patches. Each patch — a tiny square of pixels — is flattened and projected into a vector. Now your photo is a *sequence* of, say, 1,024 vectors.

**3. The vision encoder thinks.** A ViT runs self-attention over those patch vectors. Early layers find edges and strokes; later layers assemble them into 'this region is text,' 'this region is an arrow,' 'this is a box.' Out comes a refined set of image tokens rich with meaning.

**4. The bridge.** A small projector reshapes those image tokens so they sit comfortably in the LLM's token space — now they look, to the brain, like a strange new language of 'sight-words.'

**5. Fusion.** The LLM receives: `[image tokens] + [your text request]`. Self-attention lets your *words* ('which action item has no owner') attend to the relevant *image regions* (the list near the arrow).

**6. Reasoning and reading.** The model 'reads' the handwriting by recognizing the visual patterns of letters (no separate OCR engine needed), follows the flowchart arrows by attending across patches, and cross-references the action items against the names.

**7. The answer streams out.** Clean Markdown notes appear, plus: *'The action item "Email the vendor by Friday" has no owner listed.'*

## The takeaway

Notice there was **no separate handwriting recognizer, no separate diagram parser, no separate reasoning module.** One model did perception, reading, structure, and reasoning end-to-end, because all of it became tokens flowing through one attention mechanism. That seamlessness is the whole point of the multimodal revolution.
slides.speakerNotes: Walk this slowly — it's the synthesis slide. If you have a real device, do it LIVE: photograph the actual training-room whiteboard and ask a multimodal model to extract notes. The class will gasp. Ask: 'In the old world, how many separate systems would this have taken?' (OCR + layout parser + NLP + reasoning, each brittle.) Contrast that fragility with today's single fused model.
slides.marketConnection: Whiteboard-to-notes, receipt scanning, screenshot debugging, and 'read this contract PDF' are everyday 2026 use cases sold as features, all riding this exact end-to-end pipeline.

slides.title: Seeing the Code — A Multimodal Call in Practice
slides.body:
## What it actually looks like for a developer

The beauty of 2026 multimodal APIs is how little code stands between you and a model that can see. You send an image plus a question; you get reasoning back. Here is the shape of a typical request (pseudocode, provider-agnostic):

```python
import base64

# 1. Load and encode the image
with open("whiteboard.jpg", "rb") as f:
    image_b64 = base64.b64encode(f.read()).decode()

# 2. Build a multimodal message: text + image in ONE turn
messages = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [
            {"type": "text",
             "text": "Turn this whiteboard into clean meeting notes. "
                     "Which action item has no owner?"},
            {"type": "image",
             "source": {"type": "base64",
                        "media_type": "image/jpeg",
                        "data": image_b64}}
        ]
    }
]

# 3. Same call you'd make for text — the model handles the image tokens
response = client.messages.create(
    model="<a-vision-capable-model>",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=messages,
)

print(response.content[0].text)
```

## What to notice

- **Images and text live together** in one `content` array. To the model, both become tokens in the same sequence.
- **No separate vision pipeline.** You don't call an OCR service, then a layout parser, then an LLM. One call.
- **Cost note:** images consume tokens too — a high-res image can cost as much as a page of text, because it expands into many patch tokens. Bigger or more detailed images = more tokens = more cost and latency. Resize when you don't need fine detail.

## Practical patterns

- **Document Q&A:** feed page images of a PDF and ask grounded questions.
- **Visual diff/debugging:** paste a screenshot of a broken UI and ask what's wrong.
- **Structured extraction:** ask for JSON output describing what's in the image (great for pipelines).
- **Multiple images:** send several at once and ask the model to compare them.
slides.speakerNotes: Reassure the developers in the room: 'You already know how to do this — it's the same chat call, you just add an image block.' Emphasize the cost gotcha; many teams are shocked their bill jumped because images are token-hungry. Ask: 'If an image costs tokens, when would you DOWNSIZE it before sending?' (When you only need the gist, not fine print.) For non-coders, the message is: it's now trivially easy, which is exactly why it's everywhere.
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, every major API exposes vision through this same simple message format, and FinOps teams now actively track 'image token spend' as a real line item.

slides.title: What Vision-Language Models Are Great At — and Where They Stumble
slides.body:
## The strengths are genuinely remarkable

Modern multimodal models excel at:

- **Holistic description & captioning** — 'a golden retriever catching a frisbee on a beach at sunset.'
- **Visual question answering** — open-ended, conversational, with follow-ups.
- **Document & chart understanding** — reading tables, invoices, forms, dashboards, and explaining them.
- **Text in images** — robust reading of signs, handwriting, screenshots, even mildly blurry text.
- **Visual reasoning** — explaining a joke, diagnosing a diagram, comparing two product photos.
- **Grounding** — increasingly, pointing to *where* something is (bounding boxes / coordinates).

## But there are real, important limits

A good teacher names the failure modes honestly:

1. **Precise counting and spatial layout.** Ask 'how many sheep are in this dense flock?' and models often miscount. Fine spatial relations ('is the cup to the LEFT or RIGHT of the laptop?') can trip them up.
2. **Tiny details & fine print.** Small text, subtle gauges, or fine textures may be lost when the image is downsampled into patches.
3. **Hallucination, now visual.** A model may confidently describe an object that isn't there, especially if the prompt implies it should be ('describe the dog' when there's no dog).
4. **Exact reading of dense numbers.** Reading a complex spreadsheet screenshot perfectly digit-by-digit is risky — verify critical numbers.
5. **Reasoning about physics/3D.** True 3D spatial understanding and physical plausibility remain weak spots.
6. **Adversarial / out-of-distribution images.** Unusual angles, occlusion, or deliberately tricky images degrade performance.

## The practitioner's mindset

Treat a vision-language model as a **brilliant, fast, slightly impulsive intern**: extraordinary at the gist, occasionally wrong on the precise count or the fine print. Use it to draft, summarize, triage, and explain — but **verify load-bearing details** (dosages, dollar amounts, legal clauses, safety-critical readings). The skill of 2026 isn't just prompting the model; it's knowing *when to trust it*.
slides.speakerNotes: Balance the awe with sobriety — this is where you build real professional judgment. Show a counting example if you can (a bowl of many candies) and let the model miscount live; it's a memorable lesson. Ask the class: 'For YOUR job, which of these limits would be a dealbreaker, and how would you design around it?' Tie it back to Day 11/12's reliability themes: capability without verification is a liability.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026 enterprises pair vision models with verification layers (human review, rule checks, or a second model) for high-stakes tasks like insurance claims, medical pre-reads, and financial document processing.

slides.title: Market Snapshot — Multimodal AI in June 2026
slides.body:
## Multimodal is the default, not the feature

The biggest shift since 2024: **multimodal stopped being a headline and became the baseline.** When a new flagship model launches in 2026, 'it can see' is assumed; the news is *how well* it sees, how fast, and how cheaply. Text-only models feel increasingly like a previous generation.

## Where it shows up in real products

- **Visual agents that act on screens.** Models that look at a screenshot, decide where to click, and operate software (the 'computer use' wave) lean entirely on vision-language understanding.
- **Document intelligence at scale.** Invoices, contracts, claims, KYC forms, lab reports — enterprises feed page images straight into multimodal models, replacing brittle OCR pipelines.
- **Real-time voice + vision assistants.** Point your phone camera and *talk* to an assistant about what it sees — translation, shopping, accessibility for the visually impaired, live tutoring.
- **Healthcare pre-reads & triage.** Vision-language models assist (never replace) clinicians on imaging and report drafting, always under human sign-off.
- **Creative & marketing ops.** Auto-tagging, brand-safety screening, alt-text generation, and asset search powered by CLIP-style embeddings.

## The economics

Image tokens are real money. A detailed image can cost like a page of text, so 2026 teams optimize aggressively: right-sizing resolution, caching, tiling only when detail is needed, and choosing smaller vision-capable models for high-volume, low-stakes tasks. **'Image token spend' is now a tracked metric.**

## The strategic read

The moat is shifting from 'can your model see?' (everyone's can) to **'can your model see reliably, cheaply, in real time, and act on what it sees?'** Grounding (knowing *where*), agentic vision (clicking and operating), and verified high-stakes reading are the competitive frontiers.
slides.speakerNotes: Make this tangible to the room's industries. Ask each person: 'Name one document or image your job deals with daily.' Then show how a multimodal model would attack it. The strategic point to leave them with: differentiation has moved up the stack — from raw capability to reliability, cost, latency, and action. That's where business value lives in 2026.
slides.marketConnection: This entire slide IS the June 2026 market snapshot: multimodality is commoditized at the input layer, and competition has moved to reliability, real-time performance, cost-per-image, and agentic action.

slides.title: Quick Check — Did the Idea Land?
slides.body:
## Test your understanding

Answer these before moving on. Discuss with a neighbor.

**Q1.** In a Vision Transformer (ViT), what plays the role that *words* play in a text Transformer?
- A) Individual pixels
- B) Image patches (small squares), each turned into a vector
- C) Color channels
- D) Convolutional filters

**Q2.** CLIP was trained using *contrastive learning*. In one sentence, what does that mean?

**Q3.** A multimodal LLM 'sees' an image by... (choose the best answer)
- A) Calling a separate OCR program and pasting the text in
- B) Converting the image into tokens that get fed into the LLM alongside text tokens
- C) Describing the image in English first, then forgetting the pixels
- D) Running a search engine on the image

**Q4.** Why does sending a high-resolution image cost more than a small one?

**Q5.** Name two tasks vision-language models are *great* at, and two where you should *verify* the output.

**Q6.** Fill in the big idea: 'Almost any signal — text, image, audio, video — can be turned into ______, and anything that becomes ______ can ride the Transformer.'

---

### Answers

**A1:** B — image patches become tokens (an image is worth 16x16 words).

**A2:** It learns by pulling matching image–text pairs together in a shared embedding space while pushing mismatched pairs apart, across hundreds of millions of examples.

**A3:** B — the vision encoder turns the image into tokens that flow into the LLM with the text; self-attention then relates words to image regions.

**A4:** A larger/more detailed image expands into more patch tokens, and cost scales with token count (more compute, more latency).

**A5:** Great: captioning, document/chart understanding, VQA, reading text. Verify: precise counting, fine print/exact numbers, spatial layout, anything safety- or money-critical.

**A6:** tokens / tokens.
slides.speakerNotes: Run this as a fast, energetic round — call on people, let pairs debate Q2 and Q4 since those reveal real understanding. The single most important answer to drill is Q6: the universal-tokenization thesis is the heart of the whole day. If they remember nothing else, they should remember 'everything becomes tokens.'

slides.title: Recap & The Big Picture
slides.body:
## What we learned today

We watched the wall between vision and language dissolve.

- **ViT** proved an image is just a sentence of patches — the Transformer is a universal seer, no convolutions required.
- **CLIP** taught pictures and words to share one meaning-space through contrastive learning, unlocking zero-shot, describe-it-to-find-it recognition.
- **Multimodal LLMs** bolted a vision encoder onto a language brain, so all the reasoning power of an LLM instantly applied to images: captioning, VQA, document reading, visual reasoning.
- **Tokenization is the universal adapter** — audio (spectrograms), video (space-time cubes), and more all reduce to tokens the same attention engine consumes.
- We faced the **limits** honestly (counting, fine print, visual hallucination) and adopted the practitioner's mindset: brilliant gist, verify the load-bearing details.
- The **2026 market** treats multimodality as a baseline; the race is now about reliability, cost-per-image, real-time speed, and *acting* on what's seen.

## The one sentence to remember

**The Transformer turned out to be not a language engine but a *sequence* engine — and once we learned to turn sight and sound into sequences, one architecture grew every sense.**

## Why this sets up tomorrow

Today the model learned to *perceive* and *understand* media. Tomorrow it learns to *create* it — to imagine images, video, and sound from nothing but a sentence. If today was the machine growing eyes, tomorrow is the machine growing an imagination.
slides.speakerNotes: Slow down and let the synthesis land. Reread 'the Transformer is a sequence engine, not a language engine' — it reframes the entire course. Ask one final reflective question: 'If one architecture can absorb any sense, what does that tell you about where AI is heading?' Then hand them the bridge: understanding media (today) vs. generating media (tomorrow).
slides.marketConnection: The shift from understanding to generating media is precisely the commercial story of 2026's creative-AI boom — the perfect setup for Day 14.

keyTakeaways: An image is just a sequence of patch-tokens — ViT showed the Transformer can see without convolutions, given enough data.
CLIP aligns images and text in a shared embedding space via contrastive learning, enabling zero-shot, 'describe-it-to-find-it' recognition.
Multimodal LLMs fuse a vision encoder into a language model so its reasoning applies directly to images — one model, one conversation, eyes and language unified.
Tokenization is the universal adapter: audio, video, and other signals all become token sequences the same attention engine can process.
Vision-language models are brilliant at the gist but fallible on counting, fine print, and exact numbers — verify load-bearing details.
By June 2026 multimodality is a baseline, not a feature; competition has moved to reliability, cost-per-image, real-time speed, and agentic action.
marketSnapshot2026: As of June 2026, multimodal capability is the default expectation for any serious model — every flagship (Claude, GPT, Gemini) ingests images, and many handle audio and video natively. The commercial action has moved up the stack: visual agents that read screens and operate software, document-intelligence pipelines that replace brittle OCR for invoices/contracts/claims, real-time camera-plus-voice assistants (including powerful accessibility tools), and assisted medical pre-reads under human sign-off. Crucially, 'image token spend' is now a tracked cost line, so teams aggressively right-size resolution and pick smaller vision models for high-volume tasks. The differentiating moat is no longer 'can it see' — it's whether a model sees reliably, cheaply, in real time, grounds *where* things are, and can act on what it perceives.
homework: Hands-on: take three photos from your real work or life — (1) a handwritten note or whiteboard, (2) a chart/dashboard or invoice, and (3) a busy scene with many similar objects (e.g., a parking lot or a fruit bowl). Send each to a vision-capable AI and ask it to (a) extract/summarize the content and (b) do something precise (count the objects, read an exact number, locate a specific item). Write half a page: Where did it shine? Where did it stumble? Which outputs would you trust without checking, and which would you verify before acting? Reflect on how the patch-token pipeline explains the failures you saw.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow the machine stops merely seeing and starts dreaming. We dive into diffusion — the astonishing 'start with pure noise and sculpt it into a picture' trick behind Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, and Midjourney — then into text-to-video (Sora and its successors) and AI music and voice. Day 14: Machines That Imagine. Bring your wildest prompt; we're going to watch a model paint with statistics.
